# Multi-Field Evidence Synthesis

## 1. Captioning and accessibility

Captions are not merely decorative text. They are synchronized text versions of speech and meaningful non-speech audio. This means COD overlays must preserve a full caption layer and cannot replace accessibility captions.

## 2. Subtitle speed and compression

Subtitle-speed research supports the claim that high speeds can reduce complete reading and comprehension. This supports compression as a safer direction than attempting to force faster reading.

## 3. Partial captions

Partial and synchronized captioning shows that, in some contexts, comprehension can be preserved while showing far less than the full transcript. COD translates this into semantic compression for short-form overlays.

## 4. Multimedia signaling

Signaling research supports cues that highlight organization and relevant information. COD uses this through operator-first, problem-cause-fix, contrast pair, and beat-card structures.

## 5. Visual physiology

Foveated vision, peripheral crowding, and limited visual working memory explain why dense overlay text fails in cluttered vertical video. COD therefore prioritizes short phrases, low clutter, and region-safe placement.

## 6. Meaning and saliency

Meaning maps suggest attention is guided by semantic value, not only low-level visual salience. COD therefore prioritizes meaning-first overlays over purely loud visual treatments.

## 7. Dynamic subtitles and saliency prediction

Dynamic subtitles and video saliency datasets make gaze-aware placement technically plausible. COD treats placement as a proof target, not a mere layout choice.

## 8. Kinetic and affective typography

Motion and expressive typography can help when they encode structure or tone, but they can overload when used as constant decoration.

## 9. Bionic Reading

The evidence for Bionic Reading as a speed-reading method is weak. COD treats bionic styling as a sparse concept-anchor component to be tested against SCC, not as a guaranteed mechanism.
